prompt.content:
---
module: schemas_attribution
deps: [schemas_line_range]
targets: [rust]
tests:
  - name: a well-formed document with three entries validates
    given: { module: greeting, target: typescript, entries: 3 }
    expect: { entries: 3 }
  - name: an entry missing its file is rejected
    given: { entry: "{promptLines:[1,1], lines:[1,1], note:x}" }
    expect: { accepts: false }
  - name: an entry with an empty file string is rejected
    given: { entry: "{promptLines:[1,1], file:'', lines:[1,1]}" }
    expect: { accepts: false }
  - name: a non-integer line range is rejected
    given: { entry: "{promptLines:[1,1], file:a.ts, lines:[1.5,2]}" }
    expect: { accepts: false }
  - name: a single-line range normalizes to a start-equals-end pair
    given: { entry: "{promptLines:[8], file:a.ts, lines:3}" }
    expect: { promptLines: [8, 8], lines: [3, 3] }
  - name: a missing note defaults to the empty string
    given: { entry: "{promptLines:[1,1], file:a.ts, lines:[1,1]}" }
    expect: { note: "" }
  - name: parsing a non-list of entries yields an empty list
    given: { entries: "{}" }
    expect: { entries: 0 }
  - name: a malformed entry list is rejected
    given: { entries: "[{promptLines:'nope', file:a, lines:[1,2]}]" }
    expect: { accepts: false }
  - name: entries-at-body-line returns every entry whose prompt range contains the line
    given: { body_line: 3 }
    expect: { notes: ["covers trimming", "trims whitespace"] }
---
# The attribution schema: prompt-line to file-line mappings

This module defines the serde types and validators for a NAPL **attribution
document** — the record mapping ranges of a prompt's body lines to the ranges of
generated file lines they produced. It is pure: no I/O. Bring in `serde`,
`serde_json`.

## Where this code lives

The working directory is a Cargo workspace whose root manifest is written and
owned by the toolchain — leave it alone. Create this module as its own member
crate in a subdirectory named `schemas_attribution/`:
`schemas_attribution/Cargo.toml` (package name `schemas_attribution`) and
`schemas_attribution/src/lib.rs`. Touch nothing outside `schemas_attribution/`.
Ensure `cargo test` passes from the workspace root before finishing.

## Builds on the `schemas_line_range` module of this workspace

Every line range in this schema is an inclusive 1-based line range that accepts
several JSON spellings (a bare number `n` meaning `n..=n`, a one-element array
`[n]` meaning `n..=n`, or a two-element array `[a, b]`), with each endpoint
required to be an integer at least 1. That value type — and its lenient
deserializer — is the **`schemas_line_range`** module of this same workspace,
generated as a sibling member crate in the `schemas_line_range/` directory (so
`../schemas_line_range` relative to this crate). It exposes a public type
`LineRange` with public `start` and `end` fields (`u32`), a
`new(start, end)` constructor, equality, copy, and a serde `Deserialize` that
performs exactly the normalization above.

Add a path dependency on that sibling member crate in your `Cargo.toml` (depend
on the `schemas_line_range` crate by path — it lives at `../schemas_line_range`)
and use its `LineRange` for every line-range field below. Do **not** reimplement
line-range parsing here, and do not depend on any hand-written crate — depend
only on the generated sibling `schemas_line_range` member crate. This sibling
path dependency is part of the same workspace, not an external crates.io
dependency, so add it even though the general guidance is to avoid outside
dependencies.

## The types

Expose these public types:

- `AttributionEntry`: one mapping entry, deserializable, comparable, cloneable,
  with public fields:
  - `prompt_lines: LineRange` whose JSON key is **`promptLines`** (camelCase).
  - `file: String`.
  - `lines: LineRange`.
  - `note: String` defaulting to the empty string when the key is absent.
- `Attribution`: a whole document, deserializable, comparable, cloneable, with
  public fields:
  - `module: String`.
  - `target: String`.
  - `entries: Vec<AttributionEntry>` defaulting to empty when absent.

## Validation

- `validate_attribution(value: serde_json::Value) -> Result<Attribution, _>`:
  deserialize the JSON value into an `Attribution`, then validate it. `module`
  must not be empty, `target` must not be empty, and every entry's `file` must
  not be empty. A structurally malformed value (for example a line range that is
  not an integer, such as `1.5`) is a deserialization error. An entry missing
  its `file`, or with an empty `file`, is rejected. Rejection is an error, not a
  panic.
- `parse_attribution_entries(value: serde_json::Value) -> Result<Vec<AttributionEntry>, _>`:
  if the value is **not** a JSON array, return an empty list (no error).
  Otherwise deserialize it as a list of entries and validate each entry's
  non-empty `file`. A malformed list (an entry whose `promptLines` is a string,
  say) is an error.

Because line ranges deserialize leniently, a single-line spelling normalizes:
an entry with `"promptLines": [8]` and `"lines": 3` yields `prompt_lines`
`8..=8` and `lines` `3..=3`. A missing `note` yields the empty string.

## Lookup

- `entries_at_body_line(attribution: &Attribution, body_line: u32) -> Vec<&AttributionEntry>`:
  return references to every entry whose `promptLines` range contains
  `body_line` (inclusive on both ends), in document order.

For example, given a document whose entries are
`{promptLines:[2,2], note:"builds the greeting"}`,
`{promptLines:[3,4], note:"trims whitespace"}`, and
`{promptLines:[3,3], note:"covers trimming"}`, the entries at body line 3 are the
two whose ranges contain 3 ("trims whitespace" and "covers trimming"), body line
2 yields only "builds the greeting", and body line 9 yields none.

files.content:
[package]
name = "schemas_attribution"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1"
schemas_line_range = { path = "../schemas_line_range" }

[dev-dependencies]
serde_json = "1"

attribution.note: AttributionError enum + Display, error not panic

mapl.message: No changes produced — existing generated code already satisfies the full spec.
mapl.reasoning: The changes were "NO CHANGES". The coding agent reports the prompt diff was empty and the existing schemas_attribution crate already realizes every requirement (types, validators, lookup, lenient line-range normalization), with all 13 tests passing. So nothing needed to be regenerated. No-op is because the requirement was already met by existing code, not because the instruction was unintelligible.

mapl.message: Error type of validate_attribution is left as `_` — concrete error type unspecified.
mapl.reasoning: The signature is written `Result<Attribution, _>` with a placeholder error type. The prompt never says what the error type should be (a custom enum, serde_json::Error, String, a boxed error, etc.), only that malformed input is a deserialization error and validation failures are "an error, not a panic". Whatever concrete type the existing code uses was an unconstrained choice.
mapl.suggestion: Specify the error type explicitly, e.g. "returns Result<Attribution, E>" and define E (for example a String message, or a named error enum with Deserialize vs Validation variants).

mapl.message: parse_attribution_entries error type also unspecified (`_` placeholder).
mapl.reasoning: Same open question as validate_attribution: the `Result<Vec<AttributionEntry>, _>` error type is a placeholder. It is unclear whether it must match validate_attribution's error type or may differ. The existing code's choice was unconstrained by the prompt.
mapl.suggestion: State the error type and whether both validators share one error type.

mapl.message: Deserialization-vs-validation error boundary is described by example only.
mapl.reasoning: The prompt distinguishes "structurally malformed" (deserialize error, e.g. 1.5) from rejected values (empty/missing file). Both are reported as errors and the observable Result contract is the same, so callers cannot tell them apart unless the error type carries that distinction — which the prompt does not require. Worth confirming whether the two error categories must be separately identifiable.

maplContent:
module: schemas_attribution
target: rust
entries:
  - promptLines:
      - 1
      - 86
    kind: no-op
    message: No changes produced — existing generated code already satisfies the full spec.
    reasoning: 'The changes were "NO CHANGES". The coding agent reports the prompt diff was empty and the existing schemas_attribution crate already realizes every requirement (types, validators, lookup, lenient line-range normalization), with all 13 tests passing. So nothing needed to be regenerated. No-op is because the requirement was already met by existing code, not because the instruction was unintelligible.
'
  - promptLines:
      - 57
      - 57
    kind: ambiguity
    message: Error type of validate_attribution is left as `_` — concrete error type unspecified.
    reasoning: 'The signature is written `Result<Attribution, _>` with a placeholder error type. The prompt never says what the error type should be (a custom enum, serde_json::Error, String, a boxed error, etc.), only that malformed input is a deserialization error and validation failures are "an error, not a panic". Whatever concrete type the existing code uses was an unconstrained choice.
'
    suggestion: 'Specify the error type explicitly, e.g. "returns Result<Attribution, E>" and define E (for example a String message, or a named error enum with Deserialize vs Validation variants).
'
  - promptLines:
      - 64
      - 68
    kind: ambiguity
    message: parse_attribution_entries error type also unspecified (`_` placeholder).
    reasoning: "Same open question as validate_attribution: the `Result<Vec<AttributionEntry>, _>` error type is a placeholder. It is unclear whether it must match validate_attribution's error type or may differ. The existing code's choice was unconstrained by the prompt.\n"
    suggestion: State the error type and whether both validators share one error type.
  - promptLines:
      - 61
      - 62
    kind: note
    message: Deserialization-vs-validation error boundary is described by example only.
    reasoning: The prompt distinguishes "structurally malformed" (deserialize error, e.g. 1.5) from rejected values (empty/missing file). Both are reported as errors and the observable Result contract is the same, so callers cannot tell them apart unless the error type carries that distinction — which the prompt does not require. Worth confirming whether the two error categories must be separately identifiable.

---
module: schemas_attribution
deps: [schemas_line_range]
targets: [rust]
tests:
  - name: a well-formed document with three entries validates
    given: { module: greeting, target: typescript, entries: 3 }
    expect: { entries: 3 }
  - name: an entry missing its file is rejected
    given: { entry: "{promptLines:[1,1], lines:[1,1], note:x}" }
    expect: { accepts: false }
  - name: an entry with an empty file string is rejected

  - name: a non-integer line range is rejected
    given: { entry: "{promptLines:[1,1], file:a.ts, lines:[1.5,2]}" }
    expect: { accepts: false }
  - name: a single-line range normalizes to a start-equals-end pair
    given: { entry: "{promptLines:[8], file:a.ts, lines:3}" }
    expect: { promptLines: [8, 8], lines: [3, 3] }
  - name: a missing note defaults to the empty string
    given: { entry: "{promptLines:[1,1], file:a.ts, lines:[1,1]}" }
    expect: { note: "" }
  - name: parsing a non-list of entries yields an empty list
    given: { entries: "{}" }
    expect: { entries: 0 }
  - name: a malformed entry list is rejected
    given: { entries: "[{promptLines:'nope', file:a, lines:[1,2]}]" }
    expect: { accepts: false }
  - name: entries-at-body-line returns every entry whose prompt range contains the line
    given: { body_line: 3 }
    expect: { notes: ["covers trimming", "trims whitespace"] }
---

entry.message: parse_attribution_entries error type also unspecified (`_` placeholder).
entry.reasoning: Same open question as validate_attribution: the `Result<Vec<AttributionEntry>, _>` error type is a placeholder. It is unclear whether it must match validate_attribution's error type or may differ. The existing code's choice was unconstrained by the prompt.
entry.suggestion: State the error type and whether both validators share one error type.

entry.message: Deserialization-vs-validation error boundary is described by example only.
entry.reasoning: The prompt distinguishes "structurally malformed" (deserialize error, e.g. 1.5) from rejected values (empty/missing file). Both are reported as errors and the observable Result contract is the same, so callers cannot tell them apart unless the error type carries that distinction — which the prompt does not require. Worth confirming whether the two error categories must be separately identifiable.
